turns.content: I'm looking at this error: InvalidAlgorithmException. I thought since I set the algorithm to HS256 in my config, it should just validate my claims struct without issues.

That exception usually means the signature verification failed because the secret key doesn't match what was used when the token was created. You mentioned setting HS256 in your config, but did you ensure that the same key instance is being passed to both the encoder and decoder? Sometimes people define a constant for the key but then accidentally create a new one or use a different environment variable later.

Oh right, I was using serde_json::Value to deserialize my claims struct directly into the token payload without wrapping it properly. I assumed jsonwebtoken would handle that automatically since it's just JSON.

That is a common mistake when working with Rust crates like jsonwebtoken. The library expects you to build the claims struct first, then serialize it manually using serde, and finally pass that serialized string along with the secret to the verify function. You cannot just dump raw serde_json::Value into the token builder directly because the internal structure needs to match exactly what the encoder produced.

I see now, I should construct my Claims struct with headers and user data, then call serialize on it before passing it to the verification logic. But why does this keep failing even after fixing that?

After fixing the serialization step, you might be running into a refresh flow issue where the token expiration or scope claims don't match your database records. Also ensure you're not using HS384 or RS256 mixed with HS256 keys unless you explicitly configure the algorithm per operation.

That makes sense, I was trying to reuse a single key across different endpoints without validating if the algorithm changed. Can you show me how to properly set up the Claims struct with headers and user data?

You should define your own Claims struct implementing Serialize and Deserialize traits, then use jsonwebtoken::encode with that struct and the secret key. For validation, always pass the same algorithm string to decode as was used during encode, like 'HS256', otherwise you'll hit InvalidAlgorithmException again.
I'm reading about authentication and keep hearing about this Json Web Token library. Can you explain what exactly a claims struct is in that context?

A claims struct in the jsonwebtoken crate represents the set of attributes you embed inside the token payload, like an ID or email. When you encode, this structure gets serialized into JSON and then base64url encoded so it becomes part of the token string.
That sounds straightforward but I'm confused about how we actually select which algorithm to use when building the token. Is there a specific trait or method on the library that forces this?

You don't force it with a single trait; instead, you explicitly pass an Algorithm enum variant like Algorithm::Hs256 to the sign function in your code. The jsonwebtoken crate expects you to choose statically at compile time which signature method to use for signing.
I see, but what if I want to be able to switch algorithms later without rewriting my entire authentication flow? Does the library support runtime selection or validation options?

At runtime you can validate tokens by specifying an Algorithm enum variant and a secret key, but changing which algorithm is used for signing requires updating your configuration. The jsonwebtoken crate does not automatically adapt; it needs explicit parameters passed to the decode or verify functions each time.

So I'm trying to build a token refresh flow and it feels just like the OAuth 2.0 dance we used in our old Node.js app, but I want to keep everything native in Rust using the jsonwebtoken crate instead of calling out to Python or Go. The big difference is that in Rust I have to manually handle the Claims struct and make sure every field I put into the header matches what my server expects when it decodes the token back down.

That comparison makes sense because you're dealing with the same exchange of short-lived access tokens for long-lived refresh tokens, but in Rust you'll want to be explicit about which claims you allow. You should define your own Claims struct that implements the Serialize trait so you can control exactly what data lives inside the token payload. When you encode, you pass this struct along with a secret key to the jsonwebtoken library using a SignerBuilder configured for HS256 or RS256 depending on whether you're sharing secrets or using public keys.

Right, so if I'm doing a refresh flow where a user sends me their expired access token plus their ID token, how do I actually validate that the new payload hasn't been tampered with? It feels like we're just re-encoding the old thing but with a new claim added.

You are right to be cautious because a refresh flow isn't just about adding claims; you need to verify the signature on every token before you trust any field inside it. You should use the VerifierBuilder from the jsonwebtoken crate and load your public key or shared secret into it, then call verify_sign with the incoming token string. The verifier will throw an error if the algorithm doesn't match or if the signature is invalid, which stops tampered tokens before they hit your Claims struct.

Okay, so I can skip the signing step on refresh requests and just do a pure verification? But what if my server expects specific claims like 'sub' or 'scope' that aren't in the standard library defaults?

Exactly, you don't sign during validation; you only sign when issuing tokens. For those custom fields like 'scope' or 'exp', you define them in your Claims struct and ensure they match what your database returns. When decoding, pass the token to the VerifierBuilder's verify_sign method and then extract the Claims object, checking that every required field exists before you use it for authorization logic.
